text:
Last week, we set up the project and connected it to GitHub. This week we move into the first core analytical task: sourcing and preparing sports data through an API workflow in R.
We will use hoopR to pull NBA basketball data. Basketball remains our common seminar example because it gives us rich public data and a clear route into visualisation and dashboard building. The workflow, however, is transferable: in another sport, the package and variables will change, but the project logic will stay the same.
We will:

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
2 A Publishing Shortcut: _publish.sh
Last week, we committed and pushed work to GitHub using three separate Terminal commands. That works, but typing the same commands every time gets repetitive. Let’s create a small shortcut script that does it all in one step.

2.1 Create the File
In the Files pane in RStudio:

click New File
choose Text File
save it as _publish.sh in your project root (the same level as nba-analytics.Rproj)
paste the following into the file and save




_publish.sh

# _publish.sh — A shortcut to save and publish your work to GitHub.
#
# Usage (in your R terminal):
#   ./_publish.sh "Your commit message here"
#
# Example:
#   ./_publish.sh "Add cleaned NBA team and player data"

git add .
git commit -m "$1"
git push -u origin main

echo "Done! Your work has been published to GitHub."




2.2 How to Use It
From now on, whenever you want to save and publish your work, open the RStudio Terminal and run:



RStudio Terminal

./_publish.sh "Your commit message here"


Replace the text inside the quotes with a short description of what you did. For example:



RStudio Terminal

./_publish.sh "Add cleaned NBA team and player data"


That single command will stage all your changes, commit them with your message, and push everything to GitHub. You will use this throughout the rest of the module.






The underscore in _publish.sh is intentional — it keeps the file near the top of your file list and signals that it is a helper script, not part of the main project output.









If you get a permission error, run chmod +x _publish.sh in your terminal just once, and this should make _publish.sh executable.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
3 Why This Matters
Analysts are often judged on whether they can obtain and prepare their own data, not only on whether they can plot a spreadsheet someone else has already cleaned.
The workflow for today is:
documentation -> choose parameters -> pull -> inspect -> summarise -> save
That sequence is one of the key transferable habits from this module.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
4 Initial Setup
Load the libraries.



R code

library(tidyverse)
library(hoopR)


Make sure the data folders exist. We will save downloaded API data in data/raw/ and cleaned, reusable tables in data/cleaned/.



R code

dir.create("data/raw", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("data/cleaned", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
5 SportsDataverse Workflow

5.1 Reading the Documentation First
Before calling any data-sourcing function, you should read its documentation. The documentation tells you what data sources are available, what functions to call, what parameters they accept, and what kind of data they return. This saves time and avoids guesswork.

5.1.1 Start with the Package Website
Every SportsDataVerse package has a documentation website. This is the best place to start because it gives you the big picture: what data sources the package can access, what functions are available, and how to get started.
For hoopR, the getting-started guide is here: https://hoopr.sportsdataverse.org/articles/getting-started-hoopR.html
From that page, you can see the main functions the package offers (e.g. load_nba_team_box(), load_nba_player_box()), what data they return, and how to use them. Browse the package website to discover what is available before you start writing code.


5.1.2 Then Check Individual Functions in R
Once you know which function you want to use, check its detailed documentation in RStudio by typing ? followed by the function name:



R console

?load_nba_team_box


This opens the help page. The three sections to focus on are:

Usage — shows the function signature and its default parameter values.
Arguments — lists every parameter, explains what it controls, and tells you what values are valid.
Value — describes what the function returns (usually a data frame with specific columns).



5.1.3 What You Learn from the hoopR Docs
Reading the documentation for load_nba_team_box(), you discover:

It has one key parameter: seasons.
seasons accepts a numeric vector — you can request a single season (e.g. 2024) or multiple seasons (e.g. 2020:2024).
The function returns a data frame where each row is one team’s performance in one game.

Knowing this before you run the code means you can choose sensible parameter values on the first attempt.


5.1.4 Example: A Football Package
The same documentation habit applies to every sports data package. For example, worldfootballR provides football (soccer) data from sources including FBref and Transfermarkt. If you wanted to pull transfer data from Transfermarkt, you would start with the package documentation:
https://jaseziv.github.io/worldfootballR/articles/extract-transfermarkt-data.html
That page shows you which functions access Transfermarkt data, what parameters they require, and what the returned data looks like. Once you have identified the function you need, check its details in R:



R console

?tm_player_market_values


The Arguments section would tell you parameters like:

country_name — e.g. "England" for the English leagues
start_year — the year the season starts, e.g. 2023 for the 2023/24 season
league_url — optionally, a direct Transfermarkt league URL

So a call might look like:



R code

epl_values <- tm_player_market_values(
  country_name = "England",
  start_year = 2023
)








You do not need to memorise every parameter. You need to know where to look: start with the package website to see what is available, then use ?function_name in R to check the details. The documentation is always your first stop.






5.2 Step 1: Pull Team Box Score Data
load_nba_team_box() downloads team-level box score data from ESPN via the hoopR API wrapper. Each row in the result represents one team’s performance in one game, with columns for points, shooting percentages, rebounds, assists and more.



R code

nba_team_box <- load_nba_team_box(seasons = 2024)

saveRDS(nba_team_box, "data/raw/nba_2024_team_box.rds")
write_csv(nba_team_box, "data/raw/nba_2024_team_box.csv")








In hoopR, seasons = 2024 means the NBA season that ends in 2024, i.e. the 2023/24 season. This convention is common across SportsDataVerse packages.





5.3 Step 2: Inspect the Raw Data
Never summarise a dataset before checking what it contains.



R code

dim(nba_team_box)


dim() returns the number of rows and columns. This tells you how large the dataset is — for example, 2460 46 would mean 2,460 rows and 46 columns.



R code

names(nba_team_box)


names() lists every column name. Scan through this to find the variables you care about (team name, score, shooting stats, etc.).



R code

glimpse(nba_team_box)


glimpse() is the most informative single inspection command. It shows each column’s name, type (e.g. <chr>, <dbl>) and first few values — all in one view.



R code

head(nba_team_box)


head() shows the first six rows of the data frame, so you can see actual data values in context.






You can also inspect a data frame by clicking its name in the Environment panel (top-right in RStudio). This opens a spreadsheet-style viewer where you can scroll through rows and columns, sort by clicking column headers, and search for values. It is a quick way to get a feel for the data before writing any code.



Look for:

one row per team per game;
the main team identifier;
score and result columns;
the shooting, rebounding and possession-related variables you may want later.



5.4 Step 3: Prepare a Season-Level Team Table
We want one row per team, using regular-season games only. To get there, we need to group the game-level rows by team and then summarise each group into a single row of season averages.






The variables we use below (e.g. team_score, field_goals_made, total_rebounds) come from inspecting the data in the previous step. You will need to look at what columns are available in your data and decide which ones are useful for your summary. Use names() or the Environment viewer to check what is there.






R code

nba_regular <- nba_team_box |>
  filter(season_type == 2)

nba_team_stats <- nba_regular |>
  group_by(team_short_display_name) |>
  summarise(
    games = n(),
    wins = sum(team_winner == TRUE, na.rm = TRUE),
    losses = sum(team_winner == FALSE, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_pts = round(mean(team_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_fg_pct = round(
      mean(field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100, na.rm = TRUE),
      1
    ),
    avg_3pt_made = round(mean(three_point_field_goals_made, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_rebounds = round(mean(total_rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_assists = round(mean(assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_turnovers = round(mean(turnovers, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_steals = round(mean(steals, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_blocks = round(mean(blocks, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    win_pct = round(wins / games * 100, 1)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(win_pct))


A few things to note about the code above:

group_by(team_short_display_name) splits the data into separate groups, one per team. Everything inside summarise() is then calculated within each group rather than across the whole dataset.
summarise() collapses each group into a single row. The columns you create inside it (e.g. games = n(), avg_pts = round(mean(...))) define what the summary table contains.
.groups = "drop" tells R to remove the grouping structure after summarising. Without this, the data frame would remain grouped, which can cause unexpected behaviour in later operations.
mutate() adds or modifies columns without changing the number of rows. Here we use it to calculate win_pct from the wins and games columns that summarise() just created.
avg_fg_pct is calculated from field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100 because the raw data provides made and attempted counts rather than a pre-calculated percentage.

Inspect the cleaned table.



R code

print(nba_team_stats, n = 30)
summary(nba_team_stats)
colSums(is.na(nba_team_stats))




5.5 Step 4: Prepare a Player Table
We also need a player-level table for later charts and dashboard views. The same group_by() |> summarise() pattern applies — this time grouping by player name and team.






Again, the column names below (e.g. points, rebounds, field_goals_made) come from inspecting the player box score data. Run names(nba_player_box) or click the data frame in the Environment panel to see what columns are available, and choose the ones that are relevant to your analysis.






R code

nba_player_box <- load_nba_player_box(seasons = 2024)

saveRDS(nba_player_box, "data/raw/nba_2024_player_box.rds")
write_csv(nba_player_box, "data/raw/nba_2024_player_box.csv")

nba_player_stats <- nba_player_box |>
  filter(season_type == 2, minutes > 0) |>
  group_by(athlete_display_name, team_short_display_name) |>
  summarise(
    games = n(),
    avg_pts = round(mean(points, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_rebounds = round(mean(rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_assists = round(mean(assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_fg_pct = round(
      mean(field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100, na.rm = TRUE),
      1
    ),
    avg_3pt_made = round(mean(three_point_field_goals_made, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_minutes = round(mean(minutes, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(games >= 40) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_pts))





R code

head(nba_player_stats, 20)




5.6 Step 5: Save Reusable Data Files

5.6.1 What Is an .rds File?
An .rds file is R’s native format for saving a single object to disk. It preserves the R object exactly — column types, factor levels, attributes and all. When you load it back with readRDS(), you get the same object you saved.
We also save .csv versions because CSV is a universal format that can be opened in Excel, Python, or any other tool. Use .rds for your own R workflow; use .csv when sharing with others.



R code

saveRDS(nba_team_stats, "data/cleaned/nba_2024_team_stats.rds")
write_csv(nba_team_stats, "data/cleaned/nba_2024_team_stats.csv")

saveRDS(nba_player_stats, "data/cleaned/nba_2024_player_stats.rds")
write_csv(nba_player_stats, "data/cleaned/nba_2024_player_stats.csv")


Check that the files exist.



R code

list.files("data/raw")
list.files("data/cleaned")








Saving prepared data is part of the workflow, not a tidying afterthought. It means you do not need to re-run long API calls every time you want to make a chart.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
6 Independent Practice: Your Own Sport
The basketball walkthrough above is the shared seminar example, but your individual assignment will ask you to source, clean and present data for a sport of your own choosing. This is a good opportunity to get a head start.

6.1 What to Do

Pick a sport and a SportsDataVerse package (or another R package that provides sports data through an API). Some options:



Sport
Package
Documentation




WNBA / women’s college basketball
wehoop
https://wehoop.sportsdataverse.org/


NFL (American football)
nflreadr
https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/


MLB (baseball)
baseballr
https://billpetti.github.io/baseballr/


Football (soccer)
worldfootballR
https://jaseziv.github.io/worldfootballR/


F1 (motorsport)
f1dataR
https://scasanova.github.io/f1dataR/



Install the package if you have not already, and load it.
Read the documentation for the main data-loading function in that package. Use ?function_name in the Console or browse the package website. Identify:

what parameters the function requires;
what values are valid for each parameter;
what kind of data frame it returns.

Pull one season of data using the function and your chosen parameters. Save the downloaded table in data/raw/ before you clean or summarise it.
Inspect the result using dim(), names(), glimpse() and head().
Summarise the data into a clean table with one row per team (or per player, driver, etc.), keeping the variables you find most interesting.
Save both .rds and .csv versions of the cleaned table to your data/cleaned/ folder.







The individual assignment will ask you to do exactly this kind of work — source data from an API, prepare it, and build outputs from it. The more you practise now, the less you will need to figure out later. Think of this exercise as the first step of your assignment.





6.2 Suggestions for Football (worldfootballR)
If you are choosing football, the worldfootballR package gives you access to data from FBref and Understat, including advanced stats like expected goals (xG). Note that most live scraping functions are currently unreliable due to anti-bot protections — use the load_ functions (e.g., load_fb_big5_advanced_season_stats()) as the reliable standard, and be aware that coverage is generally limited to the Big 5 European leagues. For a full guide to the football data landscape, including StatsBomb event data and Understat shot-level xG, see the Football Data Landscape workbook.
Before pulling data, think about what story your dashboard will tell. The data you pull now shapes the dashboard you build later. Here are four different angles to get you started — each leads to a different kind of dashboard.






Here are my recommended resources for data visualisation:

Fundamentals of Data Visualization by Claus Wilke [link]
Financial Times Visual Vocabulary [link]
R Graph Gallery [link]
ULMS562 Chatper 4 by Shunya Kimura [link] (Python-based but has some useful videos)





6.2.1 Angle 1: League Overview — “Who Over- or Under-Performed?”
Story: Compare all teams in a league on the required measures. Which teams scored more than their xG suggested? Which defences conceded less than expected? A league-wide view is the most direct route to the assignment measures.
Dashboard idea: A league table with the required stats, scatter plots of xG vs actual goals, and bar charts ranking teams.
FBref’s standard stats table includes columns for goals, assists, xG, xAG, and progressive actions — most of what you need in one call. Inspect the result with glimpse() to see what is there.


6.2.2 Angle 2: One Team’s Season — “How Did Their Form Change?”
Story: Pick a single team and track their results across the season. Did they start strong and fade? Did a change in tactics show up in the xG numbers? This gives you a time-series narrative and works well with line charts and rolling averages.
Dashboard idea: Match-by-match results for the chosen team, with filters or a team selector. Cumulative points, goals scored/conceded over time, and xG trends.
This returns one row per match with columns for home/away teams, scores, and xG. From here, you can filter to one team and reshape — just as we did with the NBA team box scores.


6.2.3 Angle 3: Player Spotlight — “Who Stood Out?”
Story: Compare players within a league or a team. Who created the most chances? Who outperformed their xG? A player-level focus lets you tell a scouting-style story and works well with scatter plots and ranked tables.
Dashboard idea: Player ranking tables, scatter plots (e.g. xG vs actual goals, minutes vs goal contributions), with filters for team or position.
Try different stat_type values — "standard", "shooting", "passing", "defense" — to see what player-level data is available. You can pull multiple stat types and join them together.


6.2.4 Angle 4: Cross-League Comparison — “How Do Leagues Differ?”
Story: Pull the same stats for two or more leagues (e.g. WSL and NWSL, or Premier League and La Liga) and compare. Are goals more common in one league? Is xG higher? This creates a comparative narrative and works well with grouped bar charts and faceted plots.
Dashboard idea: Side-by-side league summaries, with tabs or a dropdown to switch between leagues.






worldfootballR’s live scraping functions (e.g., fb_team_match_log_stats()) can be slow and unreliable — FBref’s anti-bot protections frequently block them. Prefer load_ functions where available. When you do scrape, pull the data once, save it to .rds, and work from the saved file afterwards — exactly the workflow we practised with basketball. See the Football Data Landscape workbook for details.






6.3 Commit and Push
Once you are happy with your work, use your _publish.sh shortcut:



RStudio Terminal

./_publish.sh "Add data for [your sport]"

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
7 Transfer Checkpoint
The details are basketball-specific, but the project habit is not. For another sport, the equivalent questions would be:

what is the raw event or match-level table?
what should one cleaned team-level row represent?
what should one cleaned player, driver, batter or club row represent?
which variables need to be saved for charting and dashboards later?

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
8 Common Issues







Problem
Solution




hoopR call fails
Check your internet connection and retry


Returned data frame is very wide
Use glimpse() and select() to focus on relevant variables


Column names differ slightly
Check names() and adapt the code


Missing values appear
Use na.rm = TRUE and inspect where they occur


Package not found
Run install.packages("package_name") first


Documentation page is empty
Try the package website instead of ? — some packages document online only

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
Last week’s Shiny app was functional — a sidebar, a dropdown and a single Plotly bar chart — but it read like one chart with controls, not a dashboard. This week we stay with the same Lakers Evolution story and close that design gap. Same data, same before/after question around the 1 February 2025 Luka trade, new layer of polish.
We will keep the bar chart from last week and add three more angles on the same story:
Each visual is sketched as a static figure first, then picked up and dropped into the dashboard. The real teaching goal is the layout toolkit that shiny and bslib give us — page_sidebar(), card(), navset_card_tab(), value_box() and layout_columns(). The workflow for today is:
Only one file ever leaves this workbook — the single cleaned .rds the app reads.

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
1 Reading List

Posit Official Layouts Tutorial: [link]
Wickham, H. 2021. Mastering Shiny. [link]
The Shiny Cheat Sheet: [link]
bslib dashboards reference: [link]

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
2 Setup
We keep last week’s stack and add bslib for the layout components, tidyr for the wide-format era table the value boxes read from, and treemapify for the static tree-map sketch. The interactive tree map is built with plotly directly — no extra library needed.



R code

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
library(shiny)
library(bslib)
library(treemapify)

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
3 Three New Metrics
Before we open the app, we add three numbers to the story. Each answers a different question — together, they let us tell shot-making apart from shot-selection.







Metric
Question it answers




eFG%
Was the shooting actually effective?


xeFG%
Were the shots good ones to take? (shot quality)


eFG% − xeFG%
Did the player finish them? (shot-making)



eFG% is standard field-goal percentage, but a made 3-pointer counts as 1.5 makes — so a 5/10 shooter who took all 3s lands at 75%, not 50%.
xeFG% is the eFG% an average shooter would produce given this player’s exact shot mix. High xeFG% = easy menu (dunks, layups, open 3s). Low xeFG% = hard menu (contested pull-ups, fadeaways).
The differential (eFG% − xeFG%) is shot-making stripped of shot-selection. Positive = finishing better than the shot difficulty predicts; negative = worse. Report it in percentage points (pp), not %.






NoteHow each metric is computed





eFG% — Effective Field Goal %
A made 3 is worth 50% more than a made 2, so eFG% credits it accordingly:
eFG% = (FGM + 0.5 × 3PM) / FGA
In our data we use a compact equivalent that uses shot_value (2 or 3) and shot_made_flag (0 or 1):
efg = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n())
Worked example — a player takes 10 shots (4 RA at 3/4, 4 Mid-Range at 2/4, 2 Above-the-Break 3s at 1/2):
numerator   = 3×2 + 2×2 + 1×3 = 13
denominator = 2 × 10           = 20
eFG%        = 13 / 20          = 65%
A perfect 2-point shooter tops out at 100%; a perfect 3-point shooter at 150%.
xeFG% — Expected eFG%
Two things set a shot’s difficulty: shot_zone_basic (where) and action_type (how). We compute xeFG% in two steps.
Step 1 — baseline per (zone × action_type). Over every shot in the dataset:
shot_baseline <- lakers_shots |>
  group_by(shot_zone_basic, action_type) |>
  summarise(xefg_baseline = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()))
One number per combination, e.g. Restricted Area + Dunk Shot ≈ 0.93; Mid-Range + Fadeaway ≈ 0.33.
Step 2 — join and average per player. Attach each shot’s baseline back onto the shot file, then average across the player’s attempts:
player_xefg = mean(xefg_baseline)
Because every shot carries its own baseline, the mean is a shot-weighted average — shots the player takes often dominate the number.
Differential — eFG% − xeFG%
A simple subtraction. For the worked example with xeFG% = 50.4%:
differential = 65.0% − 50.4% = +14.6 pp









TipReading the three metrics together





What each extreme actually tells you:

eFG% — high: the player is scoring effectively. Makes are landing, and 3-pointers (if any) are getting the extra credit they deserve.
eFG% — low: not enough shots are falling. Could be hard shots, could be poor shot-making — eFG% alone cannot tell you which.
xeFG% — high: the player is taking easy shots on average — think dunks, layups, open catch-and-shoot 3s, Restricted Area finishes. Their shot selection looks comfortable.
xeFG% — low: the player is taking hard shots on average — contested pull-ups, long 2s, step-back fadeaways. Tough menu.
Differential — positive (eFG% > xeFG%): they are making more than the shot difficulty predicts. Either they are a genuinely great finisher/shooter, or they are on a hot stretch.
Differential — negative (eFG% < xeFG%): they are making less than the shot difficulty predicts. Cold stretch, poor finishing, or defensive pressure the baseline does not see.
Differential — near zero: they are making roughly what the shot difficulty predicts. Their efficiency is explained entirely by what they attempt, not by unusual shot-making.

And the pairs worth watching:

High eFG% + high xeFG% → efficient but on easy shots. Read it as “role player doing their job”, not “star shooter”.
High eFG% + low xeFG% → efficient and on hard shots. That is a star-shooter signal — the differential will be large and positive.
Low eFG% + high xeFG% → easy shots but still missing them. A red flag — the baseline says those shots should be going in.
Low eFG% + low xeFG% → hard shots and not finishing them. Often a shot-selection problem disguised as a shot-making one.

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
4 Prepare the Cleaned App Dataset
The data-prep step is the same habit as last week: do the heavy lifting here, and ship only what the app needs. The only file we will write into app/ is the finished product.



R code

luka_trade_date <- as.Date("2025-02-01")

zone_order <- c(
  "Restricted Area",
  "In The Paint (Non-RA)",
  "Mid-Range",
  "Left Corner 3",
  "Right Corner 3",
  "Above the Break 3"
)

lakers_shots <- readRDS("data/cleaned/lakers_shots.rds") |>
  mutate(
    game_date = as.Date(game_date),
    era = factor(
      if_else(game_date < luka_trade_date, "AD Era", "Luka Era"),
      levels = c("AD Era", "Luka Era")
    )
  ) |>
  filter(shot_zone_basic %in% zone_order) |>
  mutate(shot_zone_basic = factor(shot_zone_basic, levels = zone_order))

shot_baseline <- lakers_shots |>
  group_by(shot_zone_basic, action_type) |>
  summarise(xefg_baseline = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
            .groups = "drop")

lakers_shots <- lakers_shots |>
  left_join(shot_baseline, by = c("shot_zone_basic", "action_type"))


shot_baseline is the xeFG% an average shooter would produce for each (zone × action_type) combination, computed once from every shot in the dataset. Joining it back onto lakers_shots adds an xefg_baseline column to every row, so the app never has to recompute the baseline.
Save the finished table into app/.



R code

dir.create("app", showWarnings = FALSE)
saveRDS(lakers_shots, "app/lakers_shots.rds")


That is the only file the app will read this week. Your project should now look like this:
app/
  app.R
  lakers_shots.rds          <- only this file moves to the app folder
data/
  cleaned/
    lakers_shots.rds        <- the original cleaned archive stays here

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
5 Sketch the Static Visuals
Every chart the dashboard shows is going to be a chart we have already built and inspected on its own. Four sketches, four charts — two zone views, two action-type views, plus the per-era metric table.

5.1 Sketch 1: Shot-Zone Bar Chart
Reuse the chart from last week. share is the proportion of Rui’s attempts that came from each zone within each era.



R code

lakers_colours <- c("AD Era" = "#FDB927", "Luka Era" = "#552583")

rui_by_zone <- lakers_shots |>
  filter(player_name == "Rui Hachimura") |>
  count(era, shot_zone_basic) |>
  group_by(era) |>
  mutate(share = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

ggplot(rui_by_zone, aes(x = shot_zone_basic, y = share, fill = era)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = lakers_colours) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Share of attempts", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))








Figure 1: Share of attempts per zone, AD Era vs Luka Era.







5.2 Sketch 2: Zone Efficiency (Volume’s Companion)
Sketch 1 answers where did the shots come from?. The obvious follow-up is how well did they go in?. Notice the code below is almost identical to Sketch 1 — same zones on the x-axis, same era split, same Lakers colours — but the numerator changes from share of attempts to eFG% per zone. That is the teaching point: once you have built one chart, a close sibling is often ten lines away.



R code

rui_zone_eff <- lakers_shots |>
  filter(player_name == "Rui Hachimura") |>
  group_by(era, shot_zone_basic) |>
  summarise(efg      = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
            attempts = n(),
            .groups  = "drop")

ggplot(rui_zone_eff, aes(x = shot_zone_basic, y = efg, fill = era)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = lakers_colours) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "eFG%", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))








Figure 2: eFG% per zone, AD Era vs Luka Era. Same structure as Sketch 1, different metric.





Paired together, the two charts tell the classic volume + efficiency story:

Volume chart — which zones did Rui shoot from, and did the mix change?
Efficiency chart — did he make more of them from those places?

That pairing is what makes Pattern B (a tabbed card) the natural home for these two later on. Keep the attempts column on rui_zone_eff; we will use it for the Plotly tooltip inside the app.


5.3 Sketch 3: Action Families as Tree Maps
shot_zone_basic told us where; action_type tells us how. The catch: NBA shot data carries 30+ distinct action_type strings — “Driving Reverse Layup”, “Pullup Jump Shot”, “Alley Oop Dunk”, “Running Fadeaway Shot”… A bar chart across that many categories is unreadable. A tree map copes because unimportant categories shrink to thin slivers automatically; the reader’s eye lands on the big tiles first.
We will keep the original action_type strings as the tile labels — they carry information — and only use the family as the subgroup hierarchy above them. Four families, plus an Other bucket for the stragglers:
Step 1 — the family function. If a string matches more than one keyword (e.g. “Fadeaway Jump Shot” contains both Fadeaway and Jump), precedence is Dunk → Layup → Fadeaway → Jump. The first match wins.



R code

action_family_of <- function(x) {
  dplyr::case_when(
    grepl("Dunk",     x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Dunk",
    grepl("Layup",    x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Layup",
    grepl("Fadeaway", x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Fadeaway",
    grepl("Jump",     x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Jump Shot",
    TRUE                                     ~ "Other"
  )
}


Step 2 — the per-era, per-action table. One row per (era × action_family × action_type) combination.



R code

rui_family <- lakers_shots |>
  filter(player_name == "Rui Hachimura") |>
  mutate(action_family = action_family_of(action_type)) |>
  group_by(era, action_family, action_type) |>
  summarise(attempts = n(),
            efg      = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
            .groups  = "drop") |>
  group_by(era) |>
  mutate(proportion = attempts / sum(attempts)) |>
  ungroup()

rui_family


# A tibble: 78 × 6
   era    action_family action_type                 attempts   efg proportion
   <fct>  <chr>         <chr>                          <int> <dbl>      <dbl>
 1 AD Era Dunk          Alley Oop Dunk Shot                5 1       0.00383 
 2 AD Era Dunk          Cutting Dunk Shot                 48 0.938   0.0368  
 3 AD Era Dunk          Driving Dunk Shot                 25 0.76    0.0191  
 4 AD Era Dunk          Driving Reverse Dunk Shot          2 0.5     0.00153 
 5 AD Era Dunk          Dunk Shot                         21 0.857   0.0161  
 6 AD Era Dunk          Putback Dunk Shot                  5 1       0.00383 
 7 AD Era Dunk          Reverse Dunk Shot                  6 1       0.00459 
 8 AD Era Dunk          Running Alley Oop Dunk Shot        4 1       0.00306 
 9 AD Era Dunk          Running Dunk Shot                 47 0.894   0.0360  
10 AD Era Dunk          Running Reverse Dunk Shot          1 0       0.000766
# ℹ 68 more rows


Each row carries attempts, proportion and efg. The tree map sizes tiles by proportion (share within each era) so the two era sub-trees stay comparable even when one has fewer total shots, and colours tiles by efg.
Step 3 — the static sketch. treemapify gives ggplot a geom_treemap() we can drop straight onto rui_family. Facet per era so the two halves stay visually separated, and keep the scale-fill gradient on efg.



R code

ggplot(rui_family,
       aes(area    = proportion,
           fill    = efg,
           label   = action_type,
           subgroup = action_family)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "white", size = 3) +
  geom_treemap_text(colour = "white", place = "centre",
                    grow = FALSE, reflow = TRUE, min.size = 6) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                       limits = c(0, 1), name = "eFG%") +
  facet_wrap(~ era) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))








Figure 3: Rui Hachimura’s action-type tree map, AD Era vs Luka Era. Tile size = share within era, fill = eFG%.





The static sketch already tells the story: which families dominate each era, and which individual action_types inside them are getting finished. The interactive plotly version in the app will add hover tooltips and drill-down, but the shape of the chart is the one you see here.






NoteWhy a tree map (and why build it interactively)?





action_type in NBA shot data has a long tail: 30+ distinct strings, most attempted only a handful of times per player. Two common reflexes both fail:

a bar chart with 30 dodged categories crashes the x-axis labels and shrinks the bars we most want to read;
a top-N filter hides exactly the specialised shots that separate one shooter from another (a Step-Back 3, a Turnaround Fadeaway).

A tree map sidesteps both: tile area encodes share of attempts — common shots dominate, rare ones shrink to slivers — and a subgroup hierarchy (family → action_type) lets the eye land on a family first. The static sketch above nails the layout; the interactive plotly version in the app adds hover tooltips with attempts, eFG% and the change vs the other era.



rui_family is the only structure the app needs; the plotly code lives with the rest of the dashboard in the next section.


5.4 Sketch 4: The Three Headline Numbers, Per Era
The three metrics become a story when we compute them once per era and compare. That is the shape the value boxes will end up showing: the Luka Era number as the headline, with a short “▲ +4.1 pp vs AD Era” line underneath. Build the table first.



R code

rui_era_metrics <- lakers_shots |>
  filter(player_name == "Rui Hachimura") |>
  group_by(era) |>
  summarise(attempts = n(),
            efg      = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
            xefg     = mean(xefg_baseline),
            diff     = efg - xefg,
            .groups  = "drop")

rui_era_metrics


# A tibble: 2 × 5
  era      attempts   efg  xefg    diff
  <fct>       <int> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>
1 AD Era       1306 0.585 0.604 -0.0197
2 Luka Era      803 0.615 0.581  0.0339


Three columns matter downstream:

efg — the number the first value box will show.
xefg — the second value box; reads “shot quality”.
diff — the third value box; reads “shot-making above/below expectation”.

The delta shown under each value box is just the Luka row minus the AD row, expressed in percentage points. We will compute it in the app with a short helper. Having the era table in front of you now makes it obvious what those helpers need to produce.
Four charts, one pattern in common: each was built, inspected and coloured in the workbook before touching Shiny. If any of them looked wrong here, it would be obvious here — not three cards deep in an app.

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
section: 6 Build the Dashboard, Step by Step
text:
6 Build the Dashboard, Step by Step
We do not build the dashboard in one go. We start from last week’s app and add one piece at a time. After each step the app still runs — that way, if something breaks, the cause is the change you just made, not six things bundled together.
Each step below shows only the UI change (the server pieces each step needs appear in the full listing at the end). Drop each step’s code into your own app/app.R and run it before moving on.

6.1 Step 0 — Last Week’s Starting Point
Last week you had a fluidPage() with a sidebar and one Plotly bar chart. Strip it to its bones:



Step 0

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Lakers Evolution Dashboard"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput("player", "Player",
                  choices = player_choices, selected = "Rui Hachimura")
    ),
    mainPanel(
      plotlyOutput("zone_bars", height = "440px")
    )
  )
)




6.2 Step 1 — Swap to page_sidebar() + card()
bslib replaces fluidPage() + sidebarLayout() with one call that already looks like a dashboard. Frame the chart with card() so it picks up padding, a header and a border for free.



Step 1

ui <- page_sidebar(
  title   = "Lakers Evolution Dashboard",
  sidebar = sidebar(
    width = 260,
    selectInput("player", "Player",
                choices = player_choices, selected = "Rui Hachimura")
  ),
  card(
    card_header("Shot Distribution by Zone"),
    plotlyOutput("zone_bars", height = "440px")
  )
)


Same one chart, but the page now has the dashboard look.


6.3 Step 2 — Turn the Card Into a Tabbed Card
Sketches 1 and 2 are two views of the same question. navset_card_tab() lets the reader flip between them inside a single card.



Step 2

navset_card_tab(
  title = "Shot Selection by Zone",
  full_screen = TRUE,
  nav_panel("Volume",     plotlyOutput("zone_bars", height = "360px")),
  nav_panel("Efficiency", plotlyOutput("zone_eff",  height = "360px"))
)


full_screen = TRUE adds an expand button. Both outputs still react to the same input$player; the server has no idea the tabs exist.


6.4 Step 3 — Add a Headline Row of Value Boxes
value_box() is a coloured card built around one big number. layout_columns() places three in a row. Anything after value = ... renders below the number — which is where we slip in the delta line.



Step 3

layout_columns(
  col_widths = c(4, 4, 4),
  value_box(title    = "eFG%",
            value    = textOutput("efg_value"),
            textOutput("efg_delta"),
            showcase = bsicons::bs_icon("bullseye"),
            theme    = "primary"),
  value_box(title    = "xeFG% (shot quality)",
            value    = textOutput("xefg_value"),
            textOutput("xefg_delta"),
            showcase = bsicons::bs_icon("map"),
            theme    = "secondary"),
  value_box(title    = "eFG% − xeFG% (shot-making)",
            value    = textOutput("diff_value"),
            textOutput("diff_delta"),
            showcase = bsicons::bs_icon("graph-up"),
            theme    = "success")
)


Put this row above the zone card inside page_sidebar(). The server side needs one reactive (the per-era metric table) and six text outputs (three values + three deltas).


6.5 Step 4 — Add the Action-Family Tree Map Side by Side
Wrap the zone tabbed card and a new single-chart card in layout_columns(col_widths = c(6, 6)) so they share a row.



Step 4

layout_columns(
  col_widths = c(6, 6),
  navset_card_tab(
    title = "Shot Selection by Zone",
    full_screen = TRUE,
    nav_panel("Volume",     plotlyOutput("zone_bars", height = "320px")),
    nav_panel("Efficiency", plotlyOutput("zone_eff",  height = "320px"))
  ),
  card(
    card_header("Action Families — Efficiency"),
    full_screen = TRUE,
    tags$small(style = "color: #666; padding: 0 0.75rem;",
               "Tile size = share of attempts within each era, ",
               "tile colour = eFG%. Hover for details."),
    plotlyOutput("type_tree_efficiency", height = "320px")
  )
)


When the column widths add up to 12, Bootstrap places them side by side.


6.6 Step 5 — Theme and Polish
A single bs_theme() propagates the Lakers palette to every component. Narrow the sidebar so the two cards get room, and shrink the value-box typography so the headline row reads as a summary rather than the hero.



Step 5

lakers_theme <- bs_theme(
  version   = 5,
  primary   = "#552583",
  secondary = "#FDB927",
  success   = "#2E7D32"
)

vb_title <- function(x) tags$span(style = "font-size: 0.8rem;",  x)
vb_value <- function(x) tags$span(style = "font-size: 1.3rem; font-weight: 600;", x)
vb_delta <- function(x) tags$span(style = "font-size: 0.75rem;", x)
vb_icon  <- function(name) tags$span(style = "font-size: 0.4rem;",
                                     bsicons::bs_icon(name))

# in page_sidebar: theme = lakers_theme, sidebar = sidebar(width = 200, ...)
# in value_box:    wrap title/value/delta/showcase with the helpers above


Set the theme once, pass it via page_sidebar(theme = lakers_theme), and every value_box(theme = "primary"), active tab and button matches — no tags$style() hacks required.


6.7 The Finished app.R
Every step above collapses into one file. Copy this into app/app.R:



app/app.R

library(shiny)
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(bslib)

lakers_colours  <- c("AD Era" = "#FDB927", "Luka Era" = "#552583")
luka_trade_date <- as.Date("2025-02-01")

lakers_shots   <- readRDS("lakers_shots.rds")
player_choices <- sort(unique(lakers_shots$player_name))

action_family_of <- function(x) {
  case_when(
    grepl("Dunk",     x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Dunk",
    grepl("Layup",    x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Layup",
    grepl("Fadeaway", x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Fadeaway",
    grepl("Jump",     x, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "Jump Shot",
    TRUE                                     ~ "Other"
  )
}

lakers_theme <- bs_theme(
  version   = 5,
  primary   = "#552583",
  secondary = "#FDB927",
  success   = "#2E7D32"
)

vb_title <- function(x) tags$span(style = "font-size: 0.8rem;",  x)
vb_value <- function(x) tags$span(style = "font-size: 1.3rem; font-weight: 600;", x)
vb_delta <- function(x) tags$span(style = "font-size: 0.75rem;", x)
vb_icon  <- function(name) tags$span(style = "font-size: 0.4rem;",
                                     bsicons::bs_icon(name))

ui <- page_sidebar(
  title   = "Lakers Evolution Dashboard",
  theme   = lakers_theme,
  sidebar = sidebar(
    width = 200,
    selectInput("player", "Player",
                choices  = player_choices,
                selected = "Rui Hachimura"),
    p("Value boxes compare the Luka Era to the AD Era.
       The left card splits shots by court zone.
       The right card is an interactive tree map — hover any tile
       for attempts, share, eFG% and the change vs the other era.")
  ),

  # --- Pattern C: headline numbers with era deltas (slim) ---
  layout_columns(
    col_widths = c(4, 4, 4),
    value_box(title    = vb_title("eFG% (efficiency)"),
              value    = vb_value(textOutput("efg_value", inline = TRUE)),
              vb_delta(textOutput("efg_delta", inline = TRUE)),
              showcase = vb_icon("bullseye"),
              theme    = "primary"),
    value_box(title    = vb_title("xeFG% (shot quality)"),
              value    = vb_value(textOutput("xefg_value", inline = TRUE)),
              vb_delta(textOutput("xefg_delta", inline = TRUE)),
              showcase = vb_icon("map"),
              theme    = "secondary"),
    value_box(title    = vb_title("eFG% − xeFG% (shot-making)"),
              value    = vb_value(textOutput("diff_value", inline = TRUE)),
              vb_delta(textOutput("diff_delta", inline = TRUE)),
              showcase = vb_icon("graph-up"),
              theme    = "success")
  ),

  # --- Pattern D: two cards side by side ---
  layout_columns(
    col_widths = c(6, 6),

    # Pattern B: zone — Volume + Efficiency in a tabbed card
    navset_card_tab(
      title = "Shot Selection by Zone",
      full_screen = TRUE,
      nav_panel("Volume",     plotlyOutput("zone_bars", height = "320px")),
      nav_panel("Efficiency", plotlyOutput("zone_eff",  height = "320px"))
    ),

    # Action families — single-chart card, tree map of original action_types
    card(
      card_header("Action Families — Efficiency"),
      full_screen = TRUE,
      tags$small(style = "color: #666; padding: 0 0.75rem;",
                 "Tile size = share of attempts within each era, ",
                 "tile colour = eFG%. Hover for details."),
      plotlyOutput("type_tree_efficiency", height = "320px")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  player_shots <- reactive({
    lakers_shots |> filter(player_name == input$player)
  })

  # --- Era metrics (Sketch 4) ---
  era_metrics <- reactive({
    player_shots() |>
      group_by(era) |>
      summarise(efg  = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
                xefg = mean(xefg_baseline),
                diff = efg - xefg,
                .groups = "drop") |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = era,
                  values_from = c(efg, xefg, diff))
  })

  fmt_pct   <- function(x) paste0(round(x * 100, 1), "%")
  fmt_pp    <- function(x) paste0(if (x >= 0) "+" else "",
                                  round(x * 100, 1), " pp")
  fmt_delta <- function(luka, ad) {
    d <- luka - ad
    arrow <- if (d > 0) "▲" else if (d < 0) "▼" else "—"
    paste0(arrow, " ", fmt_pp(d), " vs AD (", fmt_pct(ad), ")")
  }

  # Headline values = Luka Era
  output$efg_value  <- renderText({ fmt_pct(era_metrics()$`efg_Luka Era`)  })
  output$xefg_value <- renderText({ fmt_pct(era_metrics()$`xefg_Luka Era`) })
  output$diff_value <- renderText({ fmt_pp(era_metrics()$`diff_Luka Era`)  })

  # Delta lines = Luka − AD
  output$efg_delta  <- renderText({
    m <- era_metrics(); fmt_delta(m$`efg_Luka Era`,  m$`efg_AD Era`)
  })
  output$xefg_delta <- renderText({
    m <- era_metrics(); fmt_delta(m$`xefg_Luka Era`, m$`xefg_AD Era`)
  })
  output$diff_delta <- renderText({
    m <- era_metrics(); fmt_delta(m$`diff_Luka Era`, m$`diff_AD Era`)
  })

  # --- Volume: share of attempts per zone (Sketch 1) ---
  output$zone_bars <- renderPlotly({
    plot_data <- player_shots() |>
      count(era, shot_zone_basic) |>
      group_by(era) |>
      mutate(share = n / sum(n)) |>
      ungroup()

    gg <- ggplot(plot_data,
                 aes(x = shot_zone_basic, y = share, fill = era,
                     text = paste0(era, "<br>", shot_zone_basic, "<br>",
                                   "Attempts: ", n, "<br>",
                                   "Share: ", round(share * 100, 1), "%"))) +
      geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
      scale_fill_manual(values = lakers_colours) +
      labs(x = NULL, y = "Share of attempts", fill = NULL) +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
      theme(legend.position = "top",
            axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

    ggplotly(gg, tooltip = "text")
  })

  # --- Efficiency: eFG% per zone (Sketch 2) ---
  output$zone_eff <- renderPlotly({
    plot_data <- player_shots() |>
      group_by(era, shot_zone_basic) |>
      summarise(efg      = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
                attempts = n(),
                .groups  = "drop")

    gg <- ggplot(plot_data,
                 aes(x = shot_zone_basic, y = efg, fill = era,
                     text = paste0(era, "<br>", shot_zone_basic, "<br>",
                                   "Attempts: ", attempts, "<br>",
                                   "eFG%: ", round(efg * 100, 1), "%"))) +
      geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
      scale_fill_manual(values = lakers_colours) +
      labs(x = NULL, y = "eFG%", fill = NULL) +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
      theme(legend.position = "top",
            axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

    ggplotly(gg, tooltip = "text")
  })

  # --- Action-family long table, one row per (era × family × action_type).
  #     proportion is the share of attempts *within each era*, so the two
  #     era sub-trees stay comparable even when one has fewer total shots. ---
  family_data <- reactive({
    player_shots() |>
      mutate(action_family = action_family_of(action_type)) |>
      group_by(era, action_family, action_type) |>
      summarise(attempts = n(),
                efg      = sum(shot_made_flag * shot_value) / (2 * n()),
                .groups  = "drop") |>
      group_by(era) |>
      mutate(proportion = attempts / sum(attempts)) |>
      ungroup()
  })

  # --- Build one treemap-node table per era: families at the root,
  #     original action_types as leaves. The era label is handled as a
  #     subplot title (not as a tile) so the drill-down starts at family. ---
  era_tree_nodes <- reactive({
    d <- family_data()

    wide <- d |>
      mutate(era_key = if_else(era == "AD Era", "ad", "luka")) |>
      select(-era) |>
      pivot_wider(id_cols     = c(action_family, action_type),
                  names_from  = era_key,
                  values_from = c(attempts, proportion, efg))

    enriched <- d |>
      left_join(wide, by = c("action_family", "action_type")) |>
      mutate(
        other_era        = if_else(era == "AD Era", "Luka Era", "AD Era"),
        proportion_other = if_else(era == "AD Era",
                                   coalesce(proportion_luka, 0),
                                   coalesce(proportion_ad,   0)),
        efg_other        = if_else(era == "AD Era", efg_luka, efg_ad),
        proportion_delta = proportion - proportion_other,
        efg_delta        = efg - efg_other
      )

    sign_pp <- function(x) paste0(if_else(x >= 0, "+", ""),
                                  round(x * 100, 1), " pp")

    build_one <- function(era_name) {
      e <- enriched |> filter(era == era_name)
      if (nrow(e) == 0) return(NULL)

      leaves <- e |>
        transmute(
          id      = paste(action_family, action_type, sep = "/"),
          label   = action_type,
          parent  = action_family,
          value   = proportion,
          efg_val = efg,
          hover   = paste0(
            "<b>", action_type, "</b><br>",
            "Attempts: ", attempts, "<br>",
            "Share: ", round(proportion * 100, 1), "%",
            "  (", sign_pp(proportion_delta), " vs other era)<br>",
            "eFG%: ", round(efg * 100, 1), "%",
            if_else(!is.na(efg_delta),
                    paste0("  (", sign_pp(efg_delta), " vs other era)"),
                    "")
          )
        )

      families <- e |>
        group_by(action_family) |>
        summarise(attempts_total   = sum(attempts),
                  proportion_total = sum(proportion),
                  efg_val          = weighted.mean(efg, attempts,
                                                   na.rm = TRUE),
                  .groups          = "drop") |>
        transmute(
          id      = action_family,
          label   = action_family,
          parent  = "",
          value   = proportion_total,
          efg_val = efg_val,
          hover   = paste0("<b>", action_family, "</b><br>",
                           "Attempts: ", attempts_total, "<br>",
                           "Share: ", round(proportion_total * 100, 1), "%<br>",
                           "eFG%: ", round(efg_val * 100, 1), "%")
        )

      bind_rows(families, leaves)
    }

    list(ad = build_one("AD Era"), luka = build_one("Luka Era"))
  })

  # --- Tree map: Efficiency.
  #     Two traces (AD Era, Luka Era) side by side with era as the title;
  #     tile area = share of attempts within that era;
  #     tile colour = eFG% (shared gradient); default labels show on tiles
  #     big enough to hold them, full detail is on hover. ---
  output$type_tree_efficiency <- renderPlotly({
    trees <- era_tree_nodes()
    validate(need(!is.null(trees$ad) || !is.null(trees$luka),
                  "No shots to show for this player."))

    efg_marker <- function(efg_vals) list(
      colors     = efg_vals,
      colorscale = "Viridis",
      cmin       = 0,
      cmax       = 1,
      showscale  = TRUE,
      colorbar   = list(title = list(text = "eFG%"),
                        tickformat = ".0%")
    )

    add_era_trace <- function(p, nodes, column) {
      if (is.null(nodes)) return(p)
      add_trace(p,
        type          = "treemap",
        ids           = nodes$id,
        labels        = nodes$label,
        parents       = nodes$parent,
        values        = nodes$value,
        branchvalues  = "total",
        textinfo      = "label",
        text          = nodes$hover,
        hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>",
        marker        = efg_marker(nodes$efg_val),
        domain        = list(column = column)
      )
    }

    annotations <- list(
      list(text = "<b>AD Era</b>",
           x = 0.22, y = 1.0, xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
           xanchor = "center", yanchor = "bottom",
           showarrow = FALSE, font = list(size = 13)),
      list(text = "<b>Luka Era</b>",
           x = 0.78, y = 1.0, xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
           xanchor = "center", yanchor = "bottom",
           showarrow = FALSE, font = list(size = 13))
    )

    plot_ly() |>
      add_era_trace(trees$ad,   0) |>
      add_era_trace(trees$luka, 1) |>
      layout(
        grid        = list(columns = 2, rows = 1),
        annotations = annotations,
        margin      = list(t = 60, l = 5, r = 5, b = 5)
      )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)


Run it.



R Console

shiny::runApp("app")


Each server output maps back to a sketch: zone_bars → Sketch 1, zone_eff → Sketch 2, type_tree_efficiency → Sketch 3 (enriched with a cross-era join so the hover can compare), and the six text outputs (three values + three deltas) all read from the era table in Sketch 4.

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
7 Polish That Pays Off
The last mile of a dashboard is not about the charts — it is the finishing. A short checklist to run through before calling it done:

Give every chart a card header or tab title. "Volume" and "Efficiency" are doing caption work here — short, specific, and they tell the user what is different between the two views.
Keep the colour story tight. AD Era gold, Luka Era purple. Use the same mapping in both bar charts, the trend line and — via bs_theme() — the value-box accents.
Label value boxes for humans. "eFG% − xeFG%  (shot-making)" is long, but it tells the user what the number means. The delta line below it does the comparison work without a legend.
Set a narrow sidebar. sidebar(width = 200) frees horizontal space for the charts — enough for one dropdown and a short caption, no wider.
Turn on full_screen = TRUE for any card a user might want to stare at.
Match heights thoughtfully. Sharing height = "320px" across the two side-by-side cards keeps the page rhythmic — each card is the same size, and neither one dominates.
Shrink value boxes on data-heavy pages. A value box defaults to a loud headline size. When the row sits above two tall cards, wrap the title/value/delta in tags$span(style = "font-size: ...") to keep the eye on the charts below.
Write a short sidebar caption. One paragraph telling the user what the page shows saves every future visitor a minute of puzzling.

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
8 What to Notice
Four ideas underneath the whole workbook are worth flagging:

Static first, then Shiny. Every chart in the dashboard existed as a static figure before the app was opened. Debugging charts outside Shiny is much easier than debugging inside.
Ship only the finished data product. One file, app/lakers_shots.rds, with every column the app needs. The raw cleaning code stays in the workbook.
Think in rows of cards. A dashboard is a grid of components — value boxes, cards, tabs — each doing one job. Layout is a vocabulary, not a one-off choice.
Consistency tells the story. Two colours, one font, matching labels across panels. A consistent page feels designed; an inconsistent page feels accidental.

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
text:
9 Common Issues







Problem
Solution




page_sidebar() not found
Install and load bslib


bsicons not found
install.packages("bsicons") once, then load it


Value boxes show NaN%
The selected player has no shots in one era — add req(all(c("AD Era", "Luka Era") %in% era_metrics()$era)) or guard against missing eras in the helpers


Delta says NA pp
The pivot-wider column names include a space ("efg_Luka Era") — wrap them in backticks when you index


Tree map is empty for a player with no shots
validate(need(...)) fires on empty data — not a bug, the player genuinely has zero attempts in the current filter


Big tiles show a label but small tiles don’t
That is the plotly treemap default — labels only render where they fit. Hover any tile for the full action_type, attempts, share and eFG%


Hover tooltip looks cluttered
Edit the hover string in tree_nodes() — the lines come from a single paste0() so each row is easy to drop or reorder


Clicking a tile “zooms in”
That is the plotly treemap default — the top-left corner shows a breadcrumb; click it to zoom out


One era sub-tree is empty
The player took no shots in that era — common for mid-season arrivals or departures


Tree-map tiles look the same size in both eras
Tile area = share of attempts within each era, so a player with 20 AD shots and 200 Luka shots still fills both halves — the delta vs the other era is in the hover


Volume and Efficiency tabs look identical
Check y = share in zone_bars and y = efg in zone_eff — easy to leave both pointing at the same column when copy-pasting


Card or value-box colours look generic
Check bs_theme() is passed via page_sidebar(theme = ...) and that themes are named ("primary", "secondary", "success")


Value boxes feel too loud at the top of the page
Wrap the title, value and delta content in tags$span(style = "font-size: ...") — the Polish section of the workbook shows the pattern


Sidebar is too wide
Set sidebar(width = 200) (or smaller) — narrow sidebars give the side-by-side cards room


Tabs inside a card look like plain tabs
Use navset_card_tab(), not tabsetPanel() — the former is the carded variant

title: 4. Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II
Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
The sports analytics ecosystem moves fast. Packages break, APIs change, and data providers adjust their terms of service. This workbook maps the football data landscape as it stands in 2026 and gives you a practical, working route into two of the most important open data sources available: Hudl StatsBomb and FBref/Understat via worldfootballR.
We will:

title: Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this workbook, you will be able to:

Access granular event data from StatsBomb’s free releases, including shots, passes, and pressures with (x, y) pitch coordinates.
Access aggregate season data from FBref/Understat through worldfootballR’s pre-built loader functions.
Explain the current scraping limitations that affect football data packages and adapt your workflow accordingly.

title: Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
2 Setup
Run the following in the RStudio Console to install the packages used in this workbook.



R console

# StatsBomb's official R package
install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("statsbomb/StatsBombR")

# worldfootballR — football data from FBref, Transfermarkt, and Understat
install.packages("worldfootballR")

# Core tidyverse for wrangling and visualisation
install.packages("tidyverse")


Load the libraries for the session.



R script

# --- Libraries ----
library(StatsBombR)
library(worldfootballR)
library(tidyverse)

title: Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
3 Hudl StatsBomb: Granular Play-by-Play Data
StatsBomb provides event-level data. Every pass, shot, dribble, pressure, and goalkeeper action is logged with (x, y) pitch coordinates. This is the gold standard for spatial football analysis: pitch maps, expected goals models, passing networks, and pressure heatmaps all start here.

3.1 What Is Available?
StatsBomb’s Open Data programme releases professional-grade match event data for selected competitions. As of 2026, the major releases include:

Lionel Messi’s full career across Barcelona, PSG, and Inter Miami.
FIFA World Cups (Men’s and Women’s).
Arsenal’s 2003–04 Invincibles season.
FA Women’s Super League seasons.
UEFA Euro tournaments.







StatsBomb’s free data is limited to these specific releases. You cannot pull arbitrary league seasons. For full coverage, a commercial licence is required. However, the open data is more than enough for portfolio-quality work.





3.2 Pulling StatsBomb Data
The workflow follows four steps: load the libraries, browse the available competitions, pick a competition/season, pull its match list, then pull events for a single match.

3.2.1 Load the libraries
StatsBombR provides the open data download functions. tidyverse gives us dplyr for filtering and selecting, plus the pipe (|>) for chaining steps together.



R script

library(StatsBombR)
library(tidyverse)




3.2.2 Browse the available competitions
FreeCompetitions() calls the StatsBomb open-data servers and returns a data frame with one row per competition × season combination that is currently free to use. The table has many columns; here we keep only the identifiers and names, drop duplicates with distinct(), and sort alphabetically so the list is easy to scan.



R script

# FreeCompetitions() downloads the list of open-data competitions from StatsBomb.
competitions <- FreeCompetitions()

# Inspect the competitions and seasons available.
# Use View(available_competitions) in RStudio for a spreadsheet-style view.
available_competitions <- competitions |>
  select(competition_id, competition_name, season_id, season_name) |>
  distinct() |>
  arrange(competition_name, season_name)

available_competitions




3.2.3 Select a competition and season
Once you know what is available, filter the competitions table down to the specific competition and season(s) you want to analyse. We use named variables (target_competition_name, target_seasons) so the choice is easy to change later. The if block gives a clear error message if the filter returns nothing — usually caused by a typo in the competition or season name.



R script

# Change these values if you want to use a different competition.
target_competition_name <- "La Liga"
target_seasons <- c("2020/2021")

selected_competition <- competitions |>
  filter(
    competition_name == target_competition_name,
    season_name %in% target_seasons
  )

# Stop early with a useful message if the competition/season filter is wrong.
if (nrow(selected_competition) == 0) {
  stop("No competition found. Check target_competition_name and target_seasons.")
}




3.2.4 Download the match list
FreeMatches() takes the competition/season data frame from the previous step and returns every match in those seasons, one row per match. We convert match_date to a proper Date type and sort chronologically so the earliest fixtures appear first.



R script

matches <- FreeMatches(Competitions = selected_competition) |>
  mutate(match_date = as.Date(match_date)) |>
  arrange(match_date)

# Inspect the match list in the RStudio viewer.
matches




3.2.5 Download event data for one match
get.matchFree() takes a single-row data frame (one match) and returns its full event log: every pass, shot, dribble, pressure, and so on, with (x, y) pitch coordinates where relevant. We use slice(1) here to pick the first match in the list as an example — swap in any match_id from the table above when you want to analyse a different game.



R script

# Use the first match as an example. You can replace this with any match_id
# from the table above.
selected_match <- matches |>
  slice(1)

events <- get.matchFree(selected_match)

# Event data is the main StatsBomb event-by-event table.
# Key columns: type.name, location, pass.end_location, shot.statsbomb_xg
glimpse(events)




3.2.6 Filter to shots and inspect spatial data
Event data is long and wide: one row per event, with many columns that are only populated for certain event types (e.g. shot.* columns are only filled when type.name == "Shot"). Filtering to shots is a typical first step for building shot maps or exploring xG.



R script

shots <- events |>
  filter(type.name == "Shot") |>
  select(
    player.name,
    team.name,
    location,
    shot.end_location,
    shot.statsbomb_xg,
    shot.outcome.name
  )

# Each 'location' is a list of [x, y] coordinates on a 120x80 pitch.
shots





3.3 Why This Matters for Your Portfolio
StatsBomb data lets you demonstrate that you can:

Parse and reshape complex nested JSON structures into tidy data frames.
Work with spatial coordinates to produce pitch maps and heatmaps.
Build analyses around professional metrics like xG (expected goals).

These are skills that football clubs, agencies, and analytics companies actively recruit for.

title: Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
4 worldfootballR: FBref & Understat
Where StatsBomb gives you event-level depth, worldfootballR gives you breadth. It wraps multiple upstream sources — primarily FBref (powered by StatsBomb aggregates) and Understat — into a single R interface for season-wide statistics.

4.1 FBref: Advanced Aggregate Stats
FBref is the go-to source for detailed per-player and per-team season tables: shooting, passing, possession, defensive actions, and more. The data originates from StatsBomb but is presented in aggregate form (per-90 rates, totals, percentiles) rather than event-level.


4.2 Understat: xG-Focused Match and Shot Data
Understat provides its own expected goals (xG) model with match-level and shot-level data. It is a useful complement to FBref when you want shot maps or match-by-match xG timelines without needing the full granularity of StatsBomb events.






WarningCoverage Is Limited to Top Leagues



Both FBref and Understat focus on major European leagues — typically the Big 5 (Premier League, La Liga, Bundesliga, Serie A, Ligue 1) plus selected international tournaments. If your project targets a smaller league (e.g., Eredivisie, Liga Portugal, MLS), data availability will be patchy or nonexistent through these sources. Always check coverage before committing to a project scope.








ImportantScraping Functions Are Currently Unreliable



As of 2026, most live scraping functions in worldfootballR (e.g., fb_player_scouting_report(), fb_team_match_log_stats()) are broken or intermittently blocked by FBref’s anti-bot protections. Understat scrapers (understat_league_season_shots(), etc.) are more stable but can also fail without warning.
The reliable standard is to use load_ functions. These pull from pre-scraped, cached datasets that are updated regularly and do not trigger rate limits or blocks.
# DO NOT rely on this in 2026:
# fb_player_scouting_report("https://fbref.com/...")

# USE THIS INSTEAD:
load_fb_big5_advanced_season_stats(season_end_year = 2025)
If a scraping function works today, it may not tomorrow. Build your workflow around load_ functions for reproducibility.




4.3 FBref: Using the Loader Functions

4.3.1 1. Pull a season of advanced stats
load_fb_big5_advanced_season_stats() is a pre-built loader: instead of scraping FBref live, it pulls from a regularly updated cached dataset, which is why it is reliable. Two arguments matter:

season_end_year — the calendar year the season ends in. For the 2024/25 season, use 2025.
stat_type — which stat table to return (shooting, passing, defence, etc.). We start with "standard" (goals, assists, minutes, xG).

glimpse() gives a quick column-by-column preview, which is useful because these tables often have 40+ columns with suffixes like _Standard or _Expected that indicate which FBref section the stat came from.



R script

# Pull advanced stats for the Big 5 European leagues (2024-25 season)
big5_stats <- load_fb_big5_advanced_season_stats(
  season_end_year = 2025,
  stat_type = "standard"
)

# Inspect the structure
glimpse(big5_stats)




4.3.2 2. Filter and rank players
Once the data is in memory, the usual dplyr verbs apply. Here we narrow to Premier League forwards, keep only the columns we care about, and sort by non-penalty goals. MP_Playing is minutes-played-adjusted match count; npxG_Expected is the FBref naming convention for non-penalty expected goals.



R script

# Filter to Premier League forwards and rank by non-penalty goals
big5_stats |>
  filter(Comp == "Premier League", Pos == "FW") |>
  select(Player, Squad, MP_Playing, Gls_Standard, npxG_Expected) |>
  arrange(desc(Gls_Standard)) |>
  head(15)




4.3.3 3. Switch to a different stat type
The same loader returns completely different tables depending on stat_type. Swap the argument value to pull shooting, passing, possession, defensive, or goalkeeping stats — each comes with its own set of columns. Here we pull defensive stats and rank La Liga players by tackles.



R script

# Available stat types for load_fb_big5_advanced_season_stats():
# "standard", "shooting", "passing", "passing_types",
# "gca", "defense", "possession", "playing_time",
# "misc", "keepers", "keepers_adv"

# Example: Pull defensive stats
defensive <- load_fb_big5_advanced_season_stats(
  season_end_year = 2025,
  stat_type = "defense"
)

defensive |>
  filter(Comp == "La Liga") |>
  select(Player, Squad, Tkl_Tackles, Int, Blocks_Blocks) |>
  arrange(desc(Tkl_Tackles)) |>
  head(10)





4.4 Understat: Shot-Level xG Data
Understat’s value is its shot-level xG data with match context — useful for building xG timelines, shot maps, and team-level performance trends.

4.4.1 1. Pull match-level xG results
understat_league_match_results() returns one row per match in the chosen league season, including goals scored and Understat’s own xG values for each side. The league argument takes a short code ("EPL", "La_liga", "Bundesliga", "Serie_A", "Ligue_1", "RFPL") and season_start_year takes the year the season starts in — so the 2024/25 season is 2024.



R script

# Pull match-level xG results for a league season
# Understat covers: EPL, La Liga, Bundesliga, Serie A, Ligue 1, RFPL
epl_results <- understat_league_match_results(
  league = "EPL",
  season_start_year = 2024
)

# Each row is a match with home/away xG
epl_results |>
  select(home_team, away_team, home_goals, away_goals, home_xG, away_xG) |>
  head(10)




4.4.2 2. Pull shot-level data
understat_league_season_shots() goes one level deeper: one row per shot, with its xG value, outcome, pitch coordinates, and situation (open play, set piece, counter-attack, etc.). This is the table you want for shot maps or for rebuilding season-level xG trends from the ground up. Note the team = h_team inside select() — we rename the home-team column to team on the way through to make the output easier to read.



R script

# Pull shot-level data for a specific league season
epl_shots <- understat_league_season_shots(
  league = "EPL",
  season_start_year = 2024
)

# Each row is a single shot with xG, coordinates, and result
epl_shots |>
  select(player, team = h_team, xG, result, X, Y, situation) |>
  head(10)








Understat’s (X, Y) coordinates are normalised to a 0–1 scale (proportion of pitch length and width). To plot them on a standard pitch, multiply X by the pitch length and Y by the pitch width. This is a different coordinate system from StatsBomb’s 120×80 grid — always check the documentation before mapping.

title: Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
5 Career and Portfolio Value






TipInsight: Why This Data Matters for Your Career



Even with a limited number of open seasons and the scraping challenges we have covered, these datasets are gold for employers. Here is why:

Complex data wrangling. StatsBomb’s nested JSON structure forces you to demonstrate real data engineering skills — unnesting lists, reshaping wide-to-long, and handling missing values. This is exactly what analysts do on the job.
Spatial data mastery. Pitch maps, pass networks, and pressure heatmaps built from (x, y) coordinates show that you can work with spatial data — a transferable skill that applies well beyond football.
Understanding of modern data engineering hurdles. Employers know that data access is messy. Showing that you can navigate broken APIs, rate limits, and caching strategies demonstrates professional maturity. A portfolio that uses load_ functions and documents why is more impressive than one that pretends scraping always works.
Domain storytelling. Turning raw event data into a clear tactical insight — “This team’s high press collapsed after the 60th minute” — shows you can communicate with non-technical stakeholders.

A single well-executed StatsBomb pitch map with a clear narrative will stand out more than a dozen generic bar charts. Focus on depth over breadth.

title: Navigating the Football Data Landscape
text:
6 Conclusion: Credits and Data Ethics
Open data exists because organisations choose to share it. Respecting their terms of service keeps the ecosystem alive for everyone.

6.1 Mandatory Credits
When you publish work that uses these data sources — whether on GitHub, in a portfolio, or in a presentation — you must include proper attribution.
StatsBomb:

Data provided free of charge by StatsBomb. StatsBomb data is subject to the StatsBomb Public Data User Agreement.

FBref / Sports Reference:

Data sourced from FBref.com, provided by Sports Reference. Please review the Sports Reference data use terms.

Understat:

Data sourced from Understat.com. Understat provides xG models and shot data under its own terms of use.







Add these credits to the README.md of any GitHub repository that uses this data. It takes 30 seconds and it signals to employers that you understand data governance — a detail that separates professional analysts from hobbyists.





6.2 A Note on Responsible Use

Do not hammer FBref with rapid automated requests. This is why scraping functions break — overuse triggers protections that affect the entire community.
Cache your data locally. Pull once, save to a .csv or .rds file, and work from the local copy. This is faster, reproducible, and respectful.
Check terms before publishing. Some data providers restrict commercial use or redistribution. When in doubt, link to the source rather than redistributing the raw data.
